summary: Ad for an SDE Career Tracker, framed as a 1.5cr earnings gap between two same-college grads decided in 14 weeks before placements.

comments.text: 30 lpa fresher roles number a few hundred nationally. the funnel, not the sheet, decides.
esops in that 1.8cr are paper. most never vest past a down round.
the 1.5cr math assumes he never switches jobs. one hop resets your whole curve.
survivorship framing. 1500 buyers, and we only hear from the placed ones.
the real system was starting placement prep in year two, not the tracker.
compounding cuts both ways: a wrong 30 lpa team stalls you faster than 6 lpa.
watched a batchmate out-earn me 3x. wasn't the sheet, it was one referral.
every third post on my feed is now a career tracker with a crore-shaped headline.

summary: Delhivery SWE celebrates hitting 100K LinkedIn followers, crediting consistent posting of interview experiences, roadmaps, and resources over one viral moment.

comments.text: linkedin's algorithm rewards dwell time, not likes. that's why cheat sheets outperform milestones.
100k followers converts to maybe 2k real readers. reach is mostly ghost audience.
showing up daily works until everyone does it. the feed is saturated now.
helping people and farming engagement aren't the same goal. the second usually wins.
the next 100k won't come from posting. it comes from a product behind it.
audience is rented, not owned. one policy change and the 100k evaporates.
posted to 4 likes for a year straight. the silence phase is the real one.
'bhai 5 likes bhi nahi aaye' is the true origin story of every 100k post.

summary: Skyroot CEO celebrates Vikram-1 reaching orbit on its maiden attempt and a congratulatory call from PM Modi.

comments.text: orbit on a maiden flight is rare. spacex needed four falcon 1 tries.
vikram-1 is fully private indian hardware, not an isro handoff. that's the real milestone.
the modi call gets the headline, but recurring launch cadence is what proves a company.
one orbital success isn't a business yet. cost per kg to leo is the scoreboard.
hard part starts now: reliability across ten flights, not celebration after one.
india's launch market is tiny. skyroot lives or dies on foreign smallsat contracts.
grew up watching isro launches on doordarshan. a private one hits different.
modi calling before your own investors do is a very indian success metric.

summary: Announces joining Google as a Senior Software Engineer.

comments.text: senior at google is l5, the level where scope stops being handed to you.
external senior hires ramp slower than the title suggests. the codebase is the real interview.
the title is the easy part. staying senior in google's stack rank is the job.
prestige logos plateau careers too. big scope often lives at smaller companies.
first 90 days decide your next promo. pick the project nobody wants.
google senior is a floor now, not a ceiling. the interesting work is defining what's next.
joined a faang as senior once. spent month one just finding the build command.
senior at google means you now get paged instead of pinged.

summary: Team won 1st globally in Mastercard's 2026 Intern Innovation Challenge against 653 interns, building MasterGuard, a fraud-recognition tool.

comments.text: fraud detection is a data-access problem, not a demo problem. mastercard already has the graph.
653 interns, 36 teams means most winners are decided by the pitch, not the code.
scam detection tools die on false positives. one blocked real payment loses the user.
hackathon wins rarely ship. the constraint is compliance, not creativity.
the win worth chasing is a return offer, not the trophy.
cross-timezone teamwork is the actual skill here. that transfers, masterguard won't.
won an intern hackathon once. the demo worked exactly twice, both on stage.
653 interns and every deck opened with 'leveraging ai to combat fraud'.

summary: GeeksforGeeks founder reshares a programming joke: a true programmer makes the same mistake half a dozen times, not twice.

comments.text: half a dozen is the free tier. senior devs repeat it across three companies.
the same bug six times usually means the abstraction is wrong, not the engineer.
funny, but repeating a mistake six times is a process gap, not a personality trait.
seniors don't repeat mistakes less. they build guardrails so juniors can't repeat them.
the goal isn't zero mistakes. it's making new ones instead of old ones.
linters and CI turned this joke obsolete. the machine catches your sixth mistake now.
made the same off-by-one for years. it's basically muscle memory now.
half a dozen is rookie numbers. i've got a bug with its own git blame streak.

summary: GATE-qualified engineer announces joining Hindustan Unilever as an Executive Trainee in Supply Chain, with a light take on entering corporate life.

comments.text: hul's supply chain trainee track is one of india's oldest leadership pipelines. real gem.
supply chain make means factory floor first. the mba crowd underrates that grounding.
monday mornings aren't the shift. losing the freedom to think unstructured is.
the 'student life over' framing is backwards. trainee years are the most forgiving you'll get.
the trainees who win treat the first factory posting as leverage, not exile.
fmcg ops teaches constraint math no coding job will. that compounds later.
the jump from negotiable deadlines to KPIs is a real personality change. felt it.
'feeling normal to share' is the most honest opening line on this whole platform.

summary: Announces starting as a Technical Support Engineer at Salesforce.

comments.text: support engineering is underrated. you see more production failures in a month than devs see in a year.
tse at salesforce is the fastest path to reading real distributed-system incidents cold.
'amazing culture' is what everyone types before their first on-call weekend.
support roles get typecast fast. plan the exit into dev on day one.
the engineers who use support as a launchpad document every recurring bug they touch.
customer-facing debugging teaches communication that pure coders never build. that's the real edge.
did support early in my career. still the fastest i ever learned a system.
dream job energy right up until the first sev1 ticket at 2am.

summary: Announces promotion to Assistant Manager, Talent Acquisition at Park+, thanking mentors and team.

comments.text: ta at a growth startup means you're the reason it scales or stalls. real leverage.
recruiter promotions track hiring volume. worth naming the roles you actually filled.
assistant manager often means more reqs, same headcount. the title outpaces the support.
internal promo is great until the market resets titles. portable skills beat the ladder.
the recruiters who last move from filling reqs to fixing why people leave.
ta is becoming half analytics. the ones who learn the funnel math get pulled into strategy.
got the assistant manager bump once. new title, same laptop, double the reqs.
recruiter gets promoted, still can't find where the good candidates are hiding.

summary: Promotes a course on building a book recommender using an LLM and Python, covering vector search, zero-shot classification, and sentiment analysis.

comments.text: vector search is the whole trick here. the llm is just the last mile.
a good friend's rec beats this because they know context no embedding captures.
recommenders don't fail on the model. they fail on cold start and thin data.
zero-shot classification sounds clean, drifts hard on niche genres in practice.
build this once and you've built 80% of any rag product. same primitives.
the hard version isn't recommending books, it's explaining why. that's the sellable feature.
built a recommender once. it confidently recommended the same three books to everyone.
the best book recommender is still a friend with strong opinions and no vector db.

summary: BITS Pilani grad announces joining JPMorgan Chase full-time as a Junior Analyst, reflecting on entering finance with no prior background.

comments.text: gcb emea from a non-finance background is common. banks train the finance, hire the aptitude.
the deck-building and client-call fluency is the real product of that first year, not the models.
'randomly stepped in' undersells it. bits pilani plus jpmc is not a random draw.
no-background works at analyst level. it's a ceiling later when the technical cohort catches up.
the analysts who last pick a product to own early. generalist curiosity plateaus at vp.
finance rewards pattern recognition across deals. that's a two-year grind, not an aptitude.
switched fields with zero background once. the imposter phase is real and it passes.
'randomly stepped into jpmc from bits' is doing olympic lifting on the word randomly.

summary: ServiceNow SDE-2 shares a Cisco hiring post bundled with referral links, a hackathon, an AI tool plug, and a paid DSA/HLD/LLD cohort call.

comments.text: code with cisco is a real pipeline, but the linked cohort plug is the actual ask here.
these bundled hiring posts convert because scarcity, not because the roles are special.
one post, five links, and somehow the cisco job is the least important one.
'connect for guidance' from strangers rarely converts to referrals. warm intros still win.
the real skill this teaches is spotting which linkedin post is an ad.
when hiring posts and course sales merge, the incentive isn't your job. it's the booking.
clicked a 'guidance' link like this once. went straight to a calendly for a paid call.
found the actual job somewhere between link three and the cohort booking.

summary: ASIC engineer announces leaving Meta after 2.9 years to return to Broadcom's APD division, citing MTIA experience at Meta.

comments.text: mtia is meta's in-house ai silicon. that experience is exactly why broadcom wants him back.
asic talent is moving toward custom ai chips. broadcom's apd is where that money is.
boomerang hires usually mean the first exit was for comp, not fit. nothing wrong with that.
leaving meta mid-mtia is a bet the chip ships elsewhere faster. big call.
the real signal: hardware engineers now job-hop like software. loyalty premium is gone.
custom silicon is the next talent war. broadcom rehiring meta people is the opening shot.
went back to an old company once. the boomerang move is quietly underrated.
2.9 years then a 'second innings'. cricket metaphors are load-bearing in indian tech.

summary: LinkedIn SWE intern's team won the 'Dream Big' category at the 2026 India Intern Hackathon, building Signal, an AI career-discovery tool.

comments.text: an ai discoverability tool built inside linkedin is basically shipping their own north-star metric.
intern hackathon winners rarely reach prod, but the pitch skill is the durable takeaway.
'help professionals get discovered' is the exact loop that made the feed noisy. more signal, less signal.
career companions keep launching and dying. the problem is trust, not features.
the win that matters is the return offer conversation, not the trophy.
if signal works, it optimizes everyone identically and the edge cancels out.
pitched a whiteboard sketch on stage once. terrifying, and the best kind of fun.
built an ai tool to get discovered, on the one platform already built for that.

summary: Argues LeetCode contests are ruined in 2026: solved 4/4 in 46 min, placed 586th, and found the leaderboard stuffed with new accounts cheating via LLMs.

comments.text: codeforces already ip-bans and flags rating spikes. leetcode just chooses not to.
fastest growing sport in india: alt-tab. 20 minutes for 4/4 isn't a person.
the platform won't fix this. cheaters inflate the daily active numbers investors read.
blaming leetcode misses it. once companies stopped trusting contest rank, the incentive to police it died.
llms didn't ruin contests, they exposed that timed puzzles never measured engineering.
the fix isn't bans. it's proctored or company-run rounds. public leaderboards are done.
did a contest after a break, dropped 500 spots on the same solves. felt this exactly.
161 new accounts, all 4/4 in 20 minutes. we all know the one keyboard shortcut.

summary: New grad received full-time offers from both Microsoft (Associate Consultant) and Amazon (SDE I), and chose Amazon SDE I.

comments.text: sde over associate consultant is the right call if you want to stay technical. consulting drifts you out.
amazon sde-1 attrition is brutal in year one. the offer is easy, the tenure isn't.
these weren't the same job. picking sde over consulting is a career type, not a ranking.
two offers looks like luck. it's really two teams that already saw you intern well.
the offer you decline says more than the one you take. you chose builder over advisor.
first team matters more than first logo. optimize for the manager, not the badge.
turned down a bigger logo for a better team once. still the right call.
two offers, four bullet points, one flex. respect the format.

summary: Aggregated fresher hiring post listing SDE openings at Autodesk (Bengaluru), L&T (~6.3 LPA), and Adani (5-9 LPA).

comments.text: the ctc gap here is the story. autodesk pays multiples of l&t for the same title.
6.3 lpa for a btech software role in 2026 is below inflation-adjusted 2015 pay. that's the real headline.
these listings are engagement bait. real applications go through the careers page, not a like.
'hiring freshers' posts rarely name the recruiter. no accountability, no funnel.
product companies and legacy firms now share the sde title but not the pay band. pick accordingly.
the fresher glut is why these coexist. supply lets adani offer 5 lpa and still fill seats.
applied through a list like this once. the careers page had the same role, no middleman.
6,33,333 listed to the last rupee, like that extra 33k is what seals it.

summary: Emotional appeal to recruiters to not ghost candidates and lead hiring with empathy, noting the human story behind every resume.

comments.text: recruiters ghost because ats floods them with 500 resumes per req. it's throughput, not cruelty.
the empathy gap is structural. one recruiter to 40 openings can't personally reply to anyone.
nothing says lead with empathy like a post that'll ghost its own comments.
the ask is backwards. candidates now ghost too, because the process trained them to.
kindness doesn't scale, systems do. fund a status page, not a feelings post.
ai screening will make this worse before better. the human was already the bottleneck.
got ghosted after a final round once. the silence just teaches you to ghost back.
this gets 2000 likes from the exact recruiters who'll ghost someone next tuesday.

summary: Announces joining Amazon as a Software Development Engineer, reflecting on the journey from first 'Hello World'.

comments.text: amazon sde-1 lives in the codebase, not hello world. the real onboarding is reading legacy services.
the 'hello world to here' arc skips the part that matters: you learned to debug other people's code.
hello world was easy. the real onboarding is one 4000-line file named utils.
the join post is table stakes. the two-year retention post is the one worth writing.
your first team assignment shapes the next five years more than the offer did.
the milestone isn't joining. it's the first oncall where you fix prod alone.
still remember my first prod page. hello world did not prepare me for it.
from hello world to here, and 'here' is a wiki last updated in 2019.

summary: IIIT-Hyderabad grad reflects on completing one year as an SDE at Flipkart, on growth, mistakes, and settling in.

comments.text: year one at flipkart is the big billion days trial by fire. that scale teaches more than any course.
the real year-one skill isn't code, it's learning which meeting to skip.
'still learning' at year one is expected. the honest reflection is what you'd unlearn.
one-year posts read like gratitude, but year two is where growth stalls or compounds.
the engineers who compound pick a system to own by month eighteen. familiarity is the trap.
comfort at year one is the warning sign, not the milestone. discomfort was the growth.
year one everywhere feels like this. year two is when you find out if you grew.
'still learning, still building' is corporate for please don't pip me.
